summary: Gruber details his quest to integrate Mac OS X 10.4's new Dictionary app with BBEdit, Mailsmith, and TextWrangler, since Dictionary's built-in lookup features only work in Cocoa text fields. He catalogs the limitations: no contextual menu in Carbon apps, no useful AppleScript dictionary, and broken dict:// URL support. He presents two workarounds — assigning a keyboard shortcut to the Dictionary Services menu item, and a more sophisticated AppleScript that uses GUI Scripting to automate lookups from the current word at the insertion point. He also recommends PreFab UI Browser as essential for writing GUI scripts.
key_points: Dictionary.app's lookup features (contextual menu, keyboard shortcut) only work in Cocoa NSTextView fields, excluding popular Carbon-based editors like BBEdit
The Services menu workaround is simple but requires manual text selection; GUI Scripting enables automatic current-word detection without selection
Dictionary lacks a proper AppleScript dictionary for lookups, and its dict:// URL scheme is unreliable — a clear oversight by Apple
PreFab UI Browser is practically essential for GUI scripting work, far surpassing Apple's own UI Element Inspector
BBEdit 8.2.1 later added native support for the Command-Control-D Dictionary shortcut, making Bare Bones the first third-party developer to do so
money_quotes: Dictionary's primary advantage over online dictionaries — including Sherlock's — is that its database is stored on your computer, and thus is always available; online dictionaries are useless to an offline laptop.
To be truly useful as a writing aid, you need to be able to invoke your dictionary easily from the app in which you're writing.
I'm so lazy that I don't even want to have to select a word before doing a look-up on it.
Trying to accomplish something with GUI scripting without using UI Browser is like trying to walk around blindfolded.
It's a completely valid gripe that Dictionary ought to provide a proper AppleScript command for performing look-ups, and I hope this gets addressed in a future update.
But in the meantime, GUI scripting gets the job done today.
key_insight: When Apple's tools fall short of proper integration, resourceful scripting workarounds can bridge the gap — but the need for such workarounds reveals real shortcomings in Apple's developer story.
